title: 第 47 節
en: A. E. Gough, The Vaiseshika Aphorisms of Ka'nAda, translated, Benares, 1873.

Manilal Nabubhai Dvivedi, The Tarka-Kaumudt, being an introduction to the principles of the Vaiseshika and Nyaya-philosophies byLaugakshi Bhaskara, Bombay, 1886. This is the same author to whom we owe a valuable edition of the Yogasara-sawgraha.
Windisch, liber das Nyaya-bh&shya, Leipzig, s. a.
Kesava $astri, The Nyaya-darsana with the commentary of Vatsyayana, in the Pundit, 1877, pp. 60, 109, 311, 363 (incomplete) ; see also Sibliotheca IncUca.

Mahadeo Eajaram Bodas, The Tarkasamgraha of Annambhafta, with the author's Dipika and Govardhana's Nyayabodhini, prepared by the late Rao Bahadur Yasavanta Vasadeo Athalya, and published with critical and explanatory notes, Bombay, 1897. This book reached me after these chapters on the Nyaya and Vaiseshika were written, but not too late to' enable me to profit by several of his explanations and criticisms, before they were printed.
K"y&ya-Philosopliy.

Though Nyaya has always been translated by logic, we must not imagine that the Nyaya-Sfttras are anything like our treatises on formal logic. There is, no doubt, a greater amount of space allowed to logical questions in these than in any of the other systems of Indian philosophy ; but originally the name of Nyaya would have been quite as applicable to the Purva-Mimamsa, which is actually called Nyaya in such works, for instance, as Saya^a's Nyayamala-vistara, published by Goldstiicker. Nor is logic the sole or chief end of Gotaina's philosophy. Its chief Bb 37° INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

end, like that of the other Darsanas, is salvation, the surnmum bonum which is promised to all. This summum bonum is called by Gotama NiAsreyasa, literally that which has nothing better, the non plus ultra of blessedness. This blessedness, according to the ancient commentator Vatsya•yana, is described as consisting in renunciation with regard to all the pleasures of this life, and in the non-acceptance of, or indifference to any rewards in the life to come ; as being in fact what Brahman is, without fear, without desire, without decay, and without death. Even this Brahmahood must not be an object of desire, for such desire would at once produce a kind of bondage, and prevent that perfect freedom from all fear or hope, which is to follow by itself, but should never be yearned for. This perfect state of freedom, or resignation, can, according to Gotama, be realised in one way only, namely, by knowledge, and in this case, by a knowledge of the sixteen great topics of the Nyaya-philosophy Summum Bouum.

In this respect all the six systems of philosophy are alike, they always promise to their followers or their believers the attainment of the highest bliss that can be obtained by man. The approaches leading to that bliss vary, and the character also of the promised bliss is not always the same ; yet in each of the six systems philosophy is recommended not, as with us, for the sake of knowledge, but for the highest purpose that man can strive after in this life, that is, his own salvation.

We saw that the Vedanta recognised true salvation or Moksha in the knowledge of Brahman, which knowledge is tantamount to identity with Brahman. This Brahman or God is, as the Upanishads already declare, invisible, and far beyond the reach of the ordinary faculties of our mind. But he can be learnt from revelation as contained in the Veda, and as $vetaketu was taught ' Tat tvam asi,' £ Thou art it/ every Ved&ntist is to learn in the end the same lesson, and to realise his identity with Brahman, as the fulfilment of all desires, and the surcease of all suffering (DuAkhanta).

SUMMUM BONUM. 371 The end of all suffering is likewise the object of the Samkhya-philosophy, though it is to be reached by a different road. Kapila, being a dualist, admits an objective substratum by the side of a subjective spirit or rather spirits, and he sees the cause of all suffering in the spirits' identifying themselves with what is purely objective or material. He therefore recognises the true means of destroying all bondage and regaining perfect freedom of the spirit in our distinguishing clearly between spirit and matter, between subject and object, between Purusha and Prak?"iti. Kaivalya, or aloneness, is the right name for that highest state of bliss which is promised to us by the S&mkhya-philosophy.

The Yoga-philosophy holds much the same view of the soul recovering its freedom, but it insists strongly on certain spiritual exercises by which the soul may best obtain and maintain peace and quietness, and thus free itself effectually from the illusions and sufferings of life. It also lays great stress on devotion to a Spirit, supreme among all the other spirits, whose very existence, according to Kapila, cannot be established by any of the recognised means of real knowledge, the Prama/ra/as.

Of the two Mima?ft/s&s we have seen already that the Brahma-Mimawsa or the Ved&nta recognises salvation as due to knowledge of the Brahman, which knowledge produces at once the recognition of oneself as in reality Brahman (Brahmavid Brahma eva bhavati, 'He who knows Brahman is Brahman indeed'). It is curious to observe that, while the Samkhya insists on a distinction between Purushas, the subjects, and Prakriti, all that is objective, as the only means of final beatitude, the Ved&nta on the contrary .postulates the surrendering of all distinction between the Self and the world, and between the Self and Brahman as the right means of Moksha. The roads are different, but the point reached at last is much the same.

The other Mim&ms&, that of ffaimini, diverges widely from that of B&dar&ya7?/a. It lays its chief stress on works (Karman) and their right performance, and holds that salvation may be obtained through the performance of B b 2 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
such works, if only they are performed without any desire of rewards, whether on earth or in heaven.

Lastly, the Nyaya and Yaiseshika systems, though they also aim at salvation, are satisfied with pointing out the means of it as consisting in correct knowledge, such as can only be obtained from a clear apprehension of the sixteen topics treated by Gotarna, or the six or seven categories put forward by Ka^ada. These two philosophies, agreeing as they do among themselves, seem to me to differ very characteristically from all the others in so far as they admit of nothing invisible or transcendent (Avyakta), whether corresponding to Brahman or to Prakriti. They are satisfied with teaching that the soul is different from the body, and they think that, if this belief in the body as our own is once surrendered, our sufferings, which always reach us through the body, will cease by themselves.

But while we can understand that each of the six systems of Indian philosophy may succeed in removing pain, it is very difficult to see in what that actual happiness was supposed to consist which remained after that removal.

The Vedanta speaks of Ananda, or bliss, that resides in the highest Brahman ; but the happiness to be enjoyed by the souls near the throne of Brahman, and in a kind of paradise, is not considered as final, but is assigned to a lower class only. That paradise has no attraction, and would give no real satisfaction to those who have reached the knowledge of the Highest Brahman. Their blissful knowledge is described as oneness with Brahman, but no details are added. The bliss held out by the Samkhyas also is very vague and indefinite. It can arise only from the Purusha himself, if left entirely to himself, far from all therillusions and disturbances arising from objective nature, or the works of Prakriti.

Lastly, the Apavarga (bliss) of the Nyaya and Vaiseshika systems seems entirely negative, and produced simply by the removal of false knowledge. Even the different names given to the supreme bliss promised by each system of philosophy tell us very little. Mukti and Moksha mean MEANS OF SALVATION. 373 deliverance, Kaivalya, isolation or detachment, NiAsreyasa, non plus ultra, Amrita, immortality, Apavarga, delivery Nor does the well-known Buddhist term Nirva/na help us much. We know indeed from P&/mni (Till, 2, 50) that the word was pre-Buddhistic and existed in his time. He tells us that, if used in the sense of f blown out/ the right form would be Nirvata^, such as Nirv&to vataA, ' the wind has ceased to blow/ but Nirv&^o^/a/i, 'the fire is gone out/ We cannot prove, however, that NirvS/ria was used as the technical ternx for the summum bonum in Pa/mni's time, and it does not seem to occur in the classical Upanishads. Its occurring as the title of one of the modern Upanishads makes it all the more likely that it was borrowed there from Buddhistic sources. There is one passage only, in the shorter text of the Maitreya 1 Upanishad where Nirva/ftam anus&sanam occurs, possibly meant for Nirvan&nus&sanam, the teaching of Nirvana. What should be clearly understood is that in the early Buddhistic writings also, Nirv&na does not yet mean a complete blowing out of the individual soul, but rather the blowing out and subsiding of all human passions and the peace and quietness which result from it. The meaning of complete annihilation was a later and purely philosophical meaning attached to Nirvana, and no one certainly could form an idea of what that Nirv&na was meant to be in the Buddhist Nihilistic or $unyat&-philosophy. I doubt even whether the Upanishads could have given us a description of what they conceived their highest Mukti or perfect freedom to be. In fact they confess themselves (Taitt. Up. II, 4, i) that ' all speech turns away from the bliss of Brahman, unable to reach it 2/ and when language fails, thought is not likely to fare better.
Means of Salvation.
Turning now to the means by which the Ny&ya-philosophy undertakes to secure the attainment of the 1 Sacred Books of the East, XV, p. 61.
a See a very learned article on Nirvana by Professor Satis Chandra Vidyabhushami, in the Journal of the Buddhist Text Society, VI, part i, p. 22.
bonum or Apavarga, we find them enumerated in the following list : —
The Sixteen Topics or Padartlias.

(i) PramaTia, means of knowledge; (3) Prameya, objects of knowledge ; (3) Samsaya, doubt ; (4) Prayo^ana, purpose ; (5) Drish^anta, instance ; (6) Siddhanta, established truth; (7) Avayava, premisses; (8) Tarka, reasoning; (9) Nirnaya, conclusion ; ( 10) Vacla, argumentation ; (i i) Galpa, sophistry; (i a) Vita^cZa, wrangling, cavilling; (13) Hetvabhasa, fallacies; (14) JOala, quibbles; (15) G&ti, false analogies; (16) Nigrahasthana, unfitness for arguing.

This may seem a very strange list of the topics to be treated by any philosophy, particularly by one that claims the title of Nyaya or logic. It is clear that in reality the chapters on Prama/?ia or means of knowledge, and Prameya, objects of knowledge, comprehend the whole of philosophy.
Means of Knowledge.
The four Prama^as, according to Gotama, are Pratyaksha, sensuous perception, Anumana, inference, Upam&na, comparison, and $abda, word.

Perception comes first, because inference can only begin to do its work after perception has prepared the way, and has supplied the material to which inference can be applied. Comparison is no more than a subordinate kind of inference, while the Sabda or the word, particularly that of the Veda, depends again, as we should say, on a previous inference by which the authority of the word, more particularly the revealed word, has first been established. Imperfect as this analysis of our instruments of knowledge may seem, it seems to me highly creditable to Indian philosophers that they should have understood the necessity of such an analysis on the very threshold of any system of philosophy. How many misunderstandings might have been avoided if all philosophers had recognised the necessity of such ai? introductory chapter. If we must depend for all our know, ledge, ^first on our senses, then on our combinatory and reasoning faculties, the question whether revelation falls OBJECTS OF KNOWLEDGE. 375 under the one or the other, or whether it can claim an independent authority, can far more easily be settled than if such questions are not asked in liwiine, but turn up casually whenever transcendental problems come to be treated.
Objects of Knowledge.
zh: A. E. 高夫著，《迦那陀的勝論經》(Vaiseshika Aphorisms of Ka'nAda)，譯文，貝拿勒斯，1873年。
曼尼拉爾‧納布拜（Manilal Nabubhai Dvivedi）著，《論理之光》（The Tarka-Kaumudt），係勞迦克希‧巴斯卡拉（Laugakshi Bhaskara）著關於勝論派及尼耶亞派哲學原理的導論，孟買，一八八六年出版。此作者同為我們所仰賴之《瑜伽要義總結》（Yogasara-sawgraha）寶貴版本的編者。
溫迪施，《尼耶亞註疏》(Nyaya-bhāṣya)，萊比錫，無出版年份。
克沙瓦‧沙斯特里（Kesava śastri），《尼耶亞哲學及其瓦茨雅亞那注疏》，載《博學者》（the Pundit），1877 年，第 60、109、311、363 頁（未完）；另參見《印加圖書館》（Bibliotheca Indica）。

瑪哈德奧‧埃亞傑拉姆‧博達斯，《塔爾卡桑格拉哈》(Tarkasamgraha)，附阿南巴塔(Annambhafta)作者自著的《迪皮卡》(Dipika)及歌瓦爾達那(Govardhana)的《紐亞亞博迪尼》(Nyayabodhini)，由已故紹爾‧巴哈杜爾‧亞薩凡塔‧瓦薩德歐‧阿薩爾亞(Rao Bahadur Yasavanta Vasadeo Athalya)編製，孟買，1897年出版，並附有批判性與說明性注解。本書在我撰寫關於紐亞亞派(Nyaya)與瓦伊舍什卡派(Vaiseshika)的章節後才送達我手中，但仍未過晚，使我得以在這些章節付印前，從他的若干闡釋與批評中受益。
佉耶哲學 (Kāyya-Philosophy)

雖然《尼耶亞》（Nyaya）一向被譯為邏輯學，但我們不應想像《尼耶亞經》（Nyaya-Sfttras）與我們的形式邏輯著作有任何相似之處。誠然，相較於印度哲學的其他體系，這些著作確實在邏輯問題上花費了更多篇幅；但原本「尼耶亞」這個名稱對於《前彌摩罕薩》（Purva-Mimamsa）同樣適用，且確實在某些著作中被稱為「尼耶亞」，例如戈爾茨圖克（Goldstiicker）出版的沙耶那（Saya^a）所著的《尼耶亞花鬘廣解》（Nyayamala-vistara）。再者，邏輯學並非高陶馬（Gotaina）哲學的唯一或首要目的。其首要目的……

其目的如同其他哲學派系（Darsanas）一樣，乃是救贖，即對所有人承諾的至高善（summum bonum）。這個至高善被喬達摩稱為涅槃利益（Niḥśreyasa），字面意義為「沒有更好的東西」，即福樂的無上之境。根據古代評論家瓦次雅那（Vatsyāyana）的說法，這種福樂被描述為：對此生一切快樂的捨棄，以及對來世任何回報的不接納或漠然；實際上就是梵（Brahman）的境界——無懼、無欲、無衰減、無死亡。即使這個梵的境界也不應成為欲求的對象，因為如此的欲求會立即產生某種束縛，妨礙那應當自然隨之而來、卻永遠不應被渴望的完全自由狀態。根據喬達摩的說法，這個完全自由或解脫的完美境界只能以一種方式達成，即通過知識——在此情況下，乃是對尼亞雅哲學（Nyaya-philosophy）十六個重大主題的知識。
在這一點上，所有六大哲學體系都是一致的，它們總是向追隨者或信徒許諾可以獲得人類所能達致的最高福樂。通向該福樂的途徑各不相同，所許諾的福樂性質也並非總是相同；然而在這六大體系中的每一個，哲學都不像在我們這裡那樣是為了知識而推薦的，而是為了人在今生可以追求的最高目的，即他自身的救贖。

我們看到吠檀多教派認為真正的救贖或解脫（Moksha）在於對梵天（Brahman）的知識，而這種知識等同於與梵天的同一性。正如奧義書已經宣示的，這個梵天或上帝是無形的，遠遠超越我們心靈一般官能所能及的範圍。但他可以通過包含在吠陀中的啟示而被認識；正如尸瓦達多（Svetaketu）被教導「汝即彼」（Tat tvam asi）一樣，每一位吠檀多主義者最終都必須學習同樣的功課，並實現他與梵天的同一性，作為一切願望的圓滿與所有苦難的終止（Duḥkhānta）。

371 所有苦難的終結同樣也是數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）的目標，儘管必須透過一條不同的道路才能達致。卡皮拉（Kapila）作為一位二元論者，承認在主觀的精神或者更確切地說是眾多精神之外，還存在著一個客觀的基質。他認為所有苦難的原因在於精神將自己與純粹客觀的或物質的事物相認同。因此，他認為真正毀滅一切束縛、重獲精神完美自由的手段，乃在於我們清楚地區分精神與物質、主體與客體、普魯沙（Purusha）與實性（Prakṛiti）。**凱瓦利亞**（Kaivalya）或「獨存」，正是數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）所允諾予我們的那個最高福樂境界的恰當名稱。
瑜伽哲學對靈魂恢復自由持基本相同的看法，但它強烈主張藉由某些精神修練，靈魂最能獲得並維持寧靜與安詳，從而有效地將自己從生命的幻象與痛苦中解脫。它也非常強調對一個精神的虔敬——該精神在所有其他精神中至高無上，根據迦毘羅的說法，其存在本身無法藉由任何公認的真知手段，即量（Pramā/ra/as）來確立。

在兩派彌摩罕薩（Mimamsa）中，我們已經看到婆羅門‧彌摩罕薩或吠檀多（Vedanta）將救贖視為源於對梵（Brahman）的知識，而這種知識立即產生對自我本質上即為梵的認識（*Brahmavid Brahma eva bhavati*，「知梵者即為梵」）。值得注意的是，雖然數論派（Samkhya）堅持主體性的靈我（Purusha）與客體性的自然（Prakriti）之間的區別乃是達成最終解脫唯一途徑，然而吠檀多卻相反地主張，放棄自我與世界之間、自我與梵之間的一切區別，才是達至解脫（Moksha）的正確方式。這兩條道路各異，但最終到達的境界卻大同小異。
另一派密摩差學派——該派由詹尼尼（Jaimini）所創立——與婆陀羅延那（Bādarāyaṇa）的學說相去甚遠。它主要強調業（Karman）及其正確的履行，並主張救贖可以通過婆羅門的儀式履行而獲得。
只要這些工作是在沒有對任何報酬的渴望下執行的，無論是塵世的或天堂的，就屬於此類著作。

最後，尼耶耶（Nyaya）與瓦伊舍希卡（Vaisheshika）等系統，雖然也以救贖為目標，但滿足於指出其手段乃在於正確知識——此種知識僅能由對喬答摩（Gotama）所論述之十六個議題的明確把握，或由迦那陀（Kaṇāda）所提出之六或七個範疇的把握而獲得。這兩種哲學彼此相互一致，卻似乎在一個很有特色的方面有別於其他所有派系，亦即它們不承認任何無形或超越的事物（Avyakta），無論此事物是與梵天（Brahman）或原質（Prakriti）相應的。它們滿足於教導靈魂不同於身體，並認為一旦我們放棄了將身體視為自我所有物的這種信念，那麼我們的苦難——它總是通過身體而降臨於我們——將會自行停止。
但儘管我們能夠理解印度六大哲學體系中的每一個都可能在消除痛苦方面取得成功，然而要看出在消除痛苦之後所餘留的那種實際幸福究竟應該如何界定，卻是極其困難的。

吠檀多學派論述歡喜（Ananda）——即存於至高梵天中的至福；然而，靈魂在梵天寶座周近以及在某種天堂中所能享受的幸福，並非被視為最終境界，而僅被歸為較低的等級。那個天堂對已達致至高梵天知識者毫無吸引力，也無法給予他們真正的滿足。他們至福的知識被描述為與梵天的合一（oneness with Brahman），但並未附加任何細節說明。數論派（Samkhyas）所承諾的至福亦甚為模糊籠統。此福樂唯有源自於原人（Purusha）本身，若其完全獨存，遠離一切源自客觀自然或實質自然（Prakriti）之運作所生的幻象與擾亂，方才可能生起。

最後，尼耶耶派（Nyaya）與瓦依舍什卡派（Vaiseshika）系統的阿帕瓦爾伽（Apavarga，極樂）似乎完全是消極的性質，單純由消除虛假知識而產生。每一哲學體系所許諾的至高極樂所賦予的不同名稱，對我們的啟示也甚微。穆克提（Mukti）與莫克莎（Moksha）意為**救贖之途**。373 解脫、凱瓦利亞（Kaivalya）、隔離或超越、涅槃舍瑞亞薩（NiAsreyasa）、終極之境、阿姆利塔（Amrita）、永生、阿帕瓦爾伽、解放。即便是眾所周知的佛教術語涅槃（Nirvana）也幫助不大。我們確實從帕尼尼（Pāṇini）（《提爾》2, 50）得知該詞在他的時代之前就已存在。他告訴我們，如果該詞用「吹滅」之意，正確的形式應為「涅瓦塔」（Nirvata），如同「涅瓦多瓦塔」（Nirv&to vataA），意即「風已停止吹動」；而「涅瓦多阿格尼」（Nirv&^o^/a/i），「火已熄滅」。然而，我們無法證明涅槃（Nirvāṇa）在帕尼尼時代已被作為至善之物（summum bonum）的技術術語使用，且它似乎並未出現在古典的奧義書中。它作為某部現代奧義書的標題而出現，這更加增強了它是從佛教來源借用而來的可能性。只有一處地方，在梅特瑞亞奧義書（Maitreya Upanishad）的較短文本中，出現「涅槃努沙薩那」（Nirvaftam anus&sanam），可能是指「涅槃努沙薩那」（Nirvanānusāsanam），涅槃的教導。需要明確理解的是，即使在早期的佛教著作中，涅槃也並非意味著個別靈魂的徹底吹滅，而是所有人類熱情的吹滅與平息，以及由此而生的寧靜與安樂。徹底滅絕的含義乃是後來才附加於涅槃的純粹哲學含義，而在佛教虛無主義或空性（Śunyatā）哲學中，確實無人能對涅槃究竟意謂何物形成觀念。我甚至懷疑奧義書本身是否能為我們描述它們所構想的最高穆克提或完全自由究竟是什麼。事實上，它們自己坦承（《泰帝利耶奧義書》II, 4, i），「一切言語都從梵之極樂轉身而去，無法到達它」，2 既然言語已然失效，思想不太可能有更好的表現。
救贖的途徑
現在轉向紐耶亞哲學（Nyaya-philosophy）所採用以確保達成¹的手段 ¹《東方聖書集》第十五卷，第六十一頁。
a 參見薩蒂斯‧詹德拉‧維迪亞布沙米（Satis Chandra Vidyabhushami）教授在《佛教文獻學會期刊》（Journal of the Buddhist Text Society）第六卷第一部分第二十二頁發表的一篇極富學識的涅槃（Nirvana）論文。
善（bonum）或解脫（Apavarga），我們發現它們被列舉如下：—
十六個主題或範疇（Padarthas）

（1）Pramana，知識的手段；（2）Prameya，知識的對象；（3）Samsaya，懷疑；（4）Prayojana，目的；（5）Dristanta，例證；（6）Siddhanta，既成真理；（7）Avayava，前提；（8）Tarka，推理；（9）Nirnaya，結論；（10）Vacla，論證；（11）Galpa，詭辯；（12）Vitanda，爭論、吹毛求疵；（13）Hetvabhasa，謬誤；（14）Jala，詭異之辭；（15）Gati，虛偽的類比；（16）Nigrahasthana，不適於論證。
這個題目清單對於任何哲學體系來說，特別是對於聲稱自己為尼夜耶（Nyaya）或邏輯學的體系來說，似乎顯得相當奇特。事實上，關於量度（Pramāṇa）或知識的手段，以及量度物（Prameya）或知識的對象這兩章，實際上包含了整個哲學的內容，這一點是顯而易見的。
知識的途徑
根據喬答摩（Gotama）的觀點，四種量（Prama^as）為：現量（Pratyaksha），即感官知覺；比量（Anumana），即推理；譬量（Upamāna），即比較；以及聲量（Śabda），即言詞。

知覺首先出現，因為推理只有在知覺已經開闢了道路、並供給了推理可以應用的材料之後，才能開始發揮其作用。比較不過是推理的一種從屬形式，而沙跋陀（Sabda）或言詞——尤其是吠陀的言詞——再次依賴於先前的推理，通過這種推理，言詞的權威性，更特別是啟示言詞的權威性，才得以首先確立。儘管我們對知識工具的這種分析似乎並不完善，但在我看來，印度哲學家應當認識到這樣一種分析的必要性，在任何哲學體系的門閾上就已進行分析，這一點是值得高度稱讚的。如果所有哲學家都認識到這樣分析的必要性，會有多少誤解本可避免呢？如果我們必須首先依賴我們的感官來獲得所有知識，其次依賴我們的組合和推理能力，那麼關於啟示是屬於其中之一，還是可以主張獨立的權威性，這個問題就可以遠比那種不及早提出這類問題、而是在討論超越問題時才偶然出現的做法更加容易地解決。
知識的對象
